paper_title: ID-LoRA: Identity-Driven Audio-Video Personalization with In-Context LoRA

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Existing video personalization methods preserve visual likeness but treat video and audio separately. Without access to the visual scene, audio models cannot synchronize sounds with on-screen actions; and because classical voice-cloning models condition only on a reference recording, a text prompt cannot redirect speaking style or acoustic environment. We propose ID-LoRA (Identity-Driven In-Context LoRA), which jointly generates a subject's appearance and voice in a single model, letting a text prompt, a reference image, and a short audio clip govern both modalities together. ID-LoRA adapts the LTX-2 joint audio-video diffusion backbone via parameter-efficient In-Context LoRA and, to our knowledge, is the first method to personalize visual appearance and voice in a single generative pass. Two challenges arise. Reference and generation tokens share the same positional-encoding space, making them hard to distinguish; we address this with negative temporal positions, placing reference tokens in a disjoint RoPE region while preserving their internal temporal structure. Speaker characteristics also tend to be diluted during denoising; we introduce identity guidance, a classifier-free guidance variant that amplifies speaker-specific features by contrasting predictions with and without the reference signal. In human preference studies, ID-LoRA is preferred over Kling 2.6 Pro by 73% of annotators for voice similarity and 65% for speaking style. On cross-environment settings, speaker similarity improves by 24% over Kling, with the gap widening as conditions diverge. A preliminary user study further suggests that joint generation provides a useful inductive bias for physically grounded sound synthesis. ID-LoRA achieves these results with only ~3K training pairs on a single GPU. Code, models, and data will be released.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: ID-LoRA: Identity-Driven Audio-Video Personalization with In-Context LoRA Aviad Dahan Moran Yanuka Noa Kraicer Lior Wolf Raja Giryes Tel Aviv University https://id-lora.github.io/

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Existing video personalization methods preserve visual likeness but treat video and audio separately. Without access to the visual scene, audio models cannot synchronize sounds with on-screen actions; and because classical voice-cloning models condition only on reference recording, text prompt cannot redirect speaking style or acoustic environment. Although prompt-conditioned audio models could offer such control, they lack access to the visual scene. We propose ID-LoRA (Identity-Driven In-Context LoRA), which jointly generates subjects appearance and voice in single model, letting text prompt, reference image, and short audio clip govern both modalities together. ID-LoRA adapts the LTX-2 joint audio-video diffusion backbone via parameter-efficient InContext LoRA and, to our knowledge, is the first method to personalize visual appearance and voice within single generative pass. Two challenges arise from this formulation. Reference and generation tokens share the same positional-encoding space, making them hard to distinguish; we address this with negative temporal positions, which place reference tokens in disjoint region of the RoPE space while preserving their internal temporal structure. Furthermore, speaker characteristics tend to be diluted during denoising; we introduce identity guidance, classifier-free guidance variant that amplifies speaker-specific features by contrasting predictions with and without the reference signal. In human preference studies ID-LoRA is preferred over Kling 2.6 Pro, the leading commercial unified model with voice personalization capabilities, by 73% of annotators for voice similarity and 65% for speaking style. Automatic metrics confirm these gains: on cross-environment settings, speaker similarity improves by 24% over Kling, with the gap widening as reference and target conditions diverge. preliminary user study further suggests that joint generation provides useful inductive bias for physically grounded sound synthesis. ID-LoRA achieves these results with only 3K training pairs on single GPU. 6 2 0 2 0 1 ] . [ 1 6 5 2 0 1 . 3 0 6 2 : r Equal contribution

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The field of generative media has witnessed paradigm shift, moving from static image synthesis to the creation of high-fidelity, temporally coherent video. Indeed, foundational text-to-video (T2V) models, such as the open-source Wan series Wan et al. [2025], demonstrate remarkable capabilities in generating complex scenes, and cross-modal personalization frameworks like Phantom Liu et al. [2025b] enable users to inject specific subject identities into these visual priors with high precision. However, human identity is inherently multimodal: person is defined not only by their appearance but distinctly by the timbre, cadence, and prosody of their voice. Despite the visual fidelity of modern personalization methods, the resulting content remains predominantly silent, or relies on disjointed audio solutions that fracture the illusion of reality. Current approaches to audio-visual personalization typically rely on cascaded pipelines, where video generation is strictly conditioned on previously synthesized audio (e.g., SadTalker Zhang et al. [2023], VASA-1 Xu et al. [2024b], Hallo Xu et al. [2024a]). These modular approaches share fundamental limitation: the voicecloning stage conditions only on an audio reference and transcript, ignoring the text prompt that describes the target scene. As result, if prompt requests an angry shout in windy outdoor setting but the reference audio was recorded in quiet studio, cascaded pipeline will simply propagate the studio-like acoustic characteristics and neutral speaking style, failing to follow the prompts intent. More broadly, cascaded generation prevents the prompt from jointly influencing both audio and video properties, limiting controllability over environment sounds and speaking style. While recent editing techniques like EditYourself Flynn et al. [2026] and Just-Dub-It Chen et al. [2026] have moved toward unified generation, they are fundamentally constrained to same-video settings. They edit an existing video, maintaining the original speaker settings and acoustic environment, and cannot generalize to the cross-video settings required to synthesize subject in novel context. Figure 1: Unified Audio-Visual Personalization with ID-LoRA. ID-LoRA takes reference audio clip and target first frame as input. Unlike cascaded pipelines that treat modalities separately, ID-LoRA jointly generates synchronized video and audio in single pass. This unified approach allows text prompt to simultaneously dictate novel content, such as specific speaking style and environmental acoustics (e.g., jackhammer is drilling in the background), while ensuring the subjects vocal identity and visual likeness are preserved across the generated sequence. Video examples with audio are available in the supplementary material. 2 In this work, we address unified audio-visual personalization  (Fig. 1)  : generating video clips of specific identity where appearance and voice are synthesized jointly within shared latent space. Unlike dubbing or editing approaches, our goal is to generalize to entirely new scenes. By operating within unified latent space, we aim to place specific subject into entirely novel contexts while preserving both their visual likeness and vocal identity. Because the modalities are coupled, the text prompt can simultaneously dictate the scenes visual generation, environmental acoustics, and speaking style. To achieve this, we propose ID-LoRA (Identity-Driven In-Context LoRA), method that adapts unified audio-video diffusion backbone (LTX-2 HaCohen et al. [2026]) for audio-visual personalization. By leveraging shared DiT backbone that processes audio and video latents, our model mimics the joint distribution of speakers appearance and acoustic signature, enabling identitypreserving generation in novel contexts. Crucially, we generalize the In-Context LoRA approach to allow the model to attend to the reference identity sample (a single first-frame image and short audio clip) and generate coherent outputs in novel settings. Because both modalities share the same text conditioning, the generated audio can follow prompt descriptions of the environment and speaking style rather than being locked to the reference clips acoustics, while simultaneously attending to the video modality. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: We introduce ID-LoRA, the first In-Context LoRA framework for zero-shot joint audiovideo personalization. We propose two architectural components that are key to effective identity transfer: negative temporal positions that place reference audio tokens in negative region of the RoPE positional space, cleanly separating them from target tokens while keeping the target at the pretrained positions; and identity guidance, classifier-free guidance generalization applied to the audio stream that amplifies speaker-specific features at inference time. We propose an evaluation protocol with curated splits that test different aspects of audiovisual personalization across multiple environments. We complement automatic metrics with two human evaluation paradigms: an A/B preference study and Mean Opinion Score (MOS) study evaluating environment sound interaction across diverse physical scenarios. We demonstrate through extensive experiments and human evaluations that our unified approach outperforms state-of-the-art cascaded baselines and commercial models in speaker similarity, lip synchronization, and audio prompt adherence.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: 2.1 Joint Audio-Video Generation Recent work has explored shared diffusion or flow-matching backbones that jointly model video and audio latents. AV-DiT Wang et al. [2024a] introduces an efficient dual-stream transformer for synchronized audio-video synthesis. JavisDiT Liu et al. [2025a] extends this paradigm to longer sequences with improved cross-modal attention, and Apollo Wang et al. [2026] unifies multiple audio-visual tasks, generation, editing, and inpainting, under single multi-task objective. Ovi Low et al. [2025] adopts twin-backbone architecture with dedicated 5B audio branch mirroring the visual backbone of Wan2.2. LTX-2 HaCohen et al. [2026], which serves as our backbone, employs an asymmetric 48-layer DiT with bidirectional cross-modal attention, achieving state-of-the-art joint generation quality. While these models produce temporally coherent audio-visual content, they are designed for general text-to-audio-video synthesis and lack mechanisms for multimodal identity personalization. 2.2 Identity Personalization in Video and Audio Visual Identity Personalization. Subject-driven image generation has advanced rapidly through optimization-based methods such as Textual Inversion Gal et al. [2022] and DreamBooth Ruiz et al. [2023], and through encoder-based approaches such as IP-Adapter Ye et al. [2023] and InstantID Wang et al. [2024b] that inject identity features into diffusion models. Parameter-efficient 3 fine-tuning via LoRA Hu et al. [2022] has become dominant paradigm, and In-Context LoRA (IC-LoRA) Huang et al. [2024] extends it by concatenating reference and target latents along the sequence dimension, enabling zero-shot identity transfer through self-attention without per-subject optimization. Video personalization builds on these foundations: VACE Jiang et al. [2025] provides an all-in-one video creation framework, and Phantom Liu et al. [2025b] enables cross-modal subject insertion with high fidelity. Our work elevates the IC-LoRA paradigm from visual-only technique to comprehensive audio-visual framework. Because cross-modal identity transfer introduces severe positional entanglement during denoising, we formulate negative temporal positions and propose identity guidance, successfully handling the unique challenges of cross-modal personalization. Audio Identity and Scene Control. Zero-shot voice cloning has progressed from speakerembedding-conditioned TTS to neural codec language models Wang et al. [2023], Peng et al. [2024] and flow-based approaches Wang et al. [2024c], Du et al. [2024]) that achieve high-fidelity speaker transfer. However, these models generate clean speech with no control over background sounds or acoustic environment. Text-to-audio models such as AudioLDM 2 Liu et al. [2024] produce rich environmental soundscapes from free-text descriptions but cannot preserve specific speakers identity. Audiobox Vyas et al. [2023] bridges this gap by combining voice cloning with text-described background sounds, yet it operates purely in the audio domain without access to the visual scene. Our unified approach goes further: by generating audio and video jointly, single text prompt controls visual content, environment sounds, and speaking style simultaneously, while the visual scene provides implicit conditioning for acoustic properties such as timing and reverberation. Throughout, the speakers vocal identity is preserved via the reference audio. Audio-Driven Talking Heads. related line of work generates talking-head video conditioned on driving audio signal Xu et al. [2024b,a], Zhang et al. [2023]. These methods animate source portrait to match input speech, producing lip-synchronized results, but they require pre-existing audio rather than generating it, and typically operate on tightly cropped face regions without modeling the broader scene or environment sounds. Our setting is fundamentally different: we jointly generate both the audio and the video of subject in novel, prompt-specified context. 2.3 Concurrent Work Audio-Visual Editing and Dubbing Just-Dub-It Chen et al. [2026] adapts LTX-2 for transcriptdriven video dubbing, EditYourself Flynn et al. [2026] enables transcript-based editing by inpainting the lip region, and Sync-LoRA Polaczek et al. [2025] propagates visual appearance changes across video but remains video-only with no audio branch. common thread is the same-video constraint: all three methods modify source video in place, inheriting its acoustic environment and speaker configuration, and none address cross-video personalization where the subject must be synthesized in an entirely new context with potentially different environment sounds and speaking style. ID-LoRA fills this gap by performing fully generative audio-visual personalization from only reference audio clip, first-frame image and text prompt. Identity-Aware Audio-Visual Synthesis DreamID-Omni Guo et al. [2026] introduces controllable framework for human-centric generation including multi-person scenarios, but requires 1M training samples and substantial architectural modifications. MM-Sonate Qiang et al. [2026] trains on 100M pairs across generation, editing, and animation tasks. In contrast, ID-LoRA demonstrates that parameter-efficient adaptation with only 3K pairs can achieve strong audio-visual personalization, with particular emphasis on enabling the text prompt to govern environment sounds and speaking style.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: Our goal is to generate audio-video content that faithfully preserves both the visual appearance and vocal identity of target individual, given only short reference audio and first frame. As illustrated in Fig. 2, we achieve this through ID-LoRA (Identity-Driven In-Context LoRA), which adapts pre-trained joint audio-video diffusion backbone to perform identity transfer within unified latent space. 4 Figure 2: ID-LoRA (overview). Target first frame and reference audio are encoded into latents, concatenated with noisy targets, and fed to shared LTX-2 DiT adapted with In-Context LoRA. Reference audio tokens receive negative temporal positions in the RoPE space, cleanly separating them from the target tokens. Joint text conditioning yields synchronized, identity-preserving video and audio whose acoustics follow the prompt and adapted to the new environment. 3.1 Preliminaries: Joint Audio-Video Diffusion We build upon LTX-2 HaCohen et al. [2026], joint audio-video generation model based on the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architecture. LTX-2 employs an asymmetric dual-stream transformer with 48 layers that jointly processes video and audio latents through bidirectional cross-modal attention. The video stream (14B parameters) handles spatiotemporal dynamics using 3D RoPE positional encoding, while the audio stream (5B parameters) processes temporal audio features using 1D RoPE. Both modalities are encoded into latent spaces: video pixels are compressed via Video VAE (32 spatial, 8 temporal compression), and audio waveforms are encoded through an Audio VAE that operates on mel spectrograms. 3.2 Identity-Driven In-Context LoRA In-Context LoRA (IC-LoRA) Huang et al. [2024] enables zero-shot adaptation by concatenating reference and target latents along the sequence dimension, allowing the model to learn identity correspondence through self-attention. We generalize this paradigm to the joint audio-video setting. Reference Conditioning. Given reference audio clip of the target speaker, we encode it into audio latents: = Ea(Aref) zref (1) where Ea denotes the audio VAE encoder. The reference audio latents are concatenated with the target audio latents along the sequence dimension, while the video stream uses standard text-to-video generation with first-frame conditioning, which provides strong visual anchor for face identity while allowing temporally coherent motion and synchronized audio: zinput = [ztarget ; zref ; ztarget ] (2) This audio-only in-context strategy allows the model to learn speaker identity transfer from the reference audio, while the video stream remains free to generate visuals guided by the text prompt and first frame while maintaining coherence with the audio. Negative Temporal Positions. key challenge in IC-LoRA is distinguishing reference tokens from target tokens during attention. In same-video settings such as dubbing or motion transfer, prior work assigns context tokens positional encodings identical to their target counterparts, enforcing strict spatiotemporal alignment between input and output Chen et al. [2026], Polaczek et al. [2025]. 5 However, in our cross-video personalization setting, the reference audio originates from different clip entirely and shares no temporal correspondence with the target. We therefore address this by assigning negative temporal positions to the reference audio tokens while keeping target positions positive. This creates clear separation in the positional encoding space: (3) where is the sequence length of the reference and target audio, respectively. This approach preserves the relative temporal structure within the reference while clearly demarcating reference from target. ttarget [0, Ttarget] tref [Tref , 0), 3.3 Training Objective We train ID-LoRA using the standard diffusion denoising objective. Given reference audio clip and target video-audio clip from the same speaker, the model learns to denoise the target latents while attending to the reference: (cid:104) = Et,ϵ ϵ ϵθ(zinput , t, c)2 2 (cid:105) (4) where ϵθ is the denoising network with LoRA parameters, and includes text conditioning describing the target scene. 3.4 Identity-Guided Inference At inference time, we introduce Identity Guidance, classifier-free guidance variant that steers generation toward better identity preservation. We compute two forward passes: one with reference conditioning and one without, then extrapolate: ˆϵ = ϵuncond θ + sid (ϵref (5) where sid is the identity guidance scale. Identity guidance follows the same principle as classifierfree guidance, but applied to the reference audio rather than the text prompt. Standard CFG extrapolates away from the unconditional prediction to amplify text influence; identity guidance similarly extrapolates between the unconditional and reference-conditioned predictions, amplifying identityspecific features, vocal timbre, speaking rhythm, pronunciation, while leaving scene content and environment sounds to be governed by the text prompt. θ ϵuncond θ )

sections.title: 4 Experimental Settings
sections.content: 4.1 Datasets We train ID-LoRA on CelebV-HQ Zhu et al. [2022] and TalkVid Chen et al. [2025], maintaining separate checkpoints for each dataset. Both datasets undergo shared preprocessing pipeline consisting of segmentation, captioning, speaker clustering, and source separation of reference audio to avoid environment leakage. For automatic evaluation, we manually curate test set of 120 video pairs drawn from 63 held-out speakers across both datasets. The CelebV-HQ portion is divided into an easy (same-video) split of 36 videos spanning 14 speakers and hard (cross-video) split of 35 videos spanning 8 speakers. The TalkVid portion comprises 49 videos from 41 held-out speakers. All captions manually verified. To assess cross-dataset generalization, we also evaluate the CelebV-HQ checkpoint on the TalkVid test set (see Sec. 5.2). Full preprocessing and split details are provided in the supplementary material. 4.2 Baselines We compare ID-LoRA against three cascaded pipelines that pair zero-shot voice-cloning model (CosyVoice 3.0 Du et al. [2025], VoiceCraft Peng et al. [2024], or ElevenLabs ElevenLabs [2026]) with WAN2.2 Wan et al. [2025] as the video backbone. ElevenLabs supports built-in emotion and style control via automatic prompt enhancement, making it the strongest cascaded baseline for speaking style adherence. We also compare against Kling 2.6 Pro Kuaishou Technology [2026], closed-source commercial model that generates talking-face video with voice cloning in unified pipeline. For both ElevenLabs and Kling, prompts were adapted following each platforms official guidelines. Per-baseline details are provided in the supplementary material. 6 Method Spk Sim Face Sim LSE-D LSE-C CLAP WER E - H - V T CosyVoice 3.0 + WAN2.2 VoiceCraft + WAN2.2 ElevenLabs + WAN2.2 Kling 2.6 Pro ID-LoRA (Ours) CosyVoice 3.0 + WAN2.2 VoiceCraft + WAN2.2 ElevenLabs + WAN2.2 Kling 2.6 Pro ID-LoRA (Ours) CosyVoice 3.0 + WAN2.2 VoiceCraft + WAN2.2 ElevenLabs + WAN2.2 Kling 2.6 Pro ID-LoRA (Ours) ID-LoRA (Celeb TalkVid) 0.510 0.412 0.413 0.487 0.573 0.391 0.344 0.357 0.385 0. 0.579 0.457 0.491 0.506 0.599 0.595 0.886 0.879 0.879 0.847 0.870 0.890 0.892 0.894 0.854 0.874 0.770 0.773 0.772 0.754 0.772 0. 11.27 10.99 11.98 10.24 8.20 11.40 10.60 11.86 9.49 8.49 12.34 12.15 12.42 11.59 10.62 10.32 1.53 1.48 1.51 3.01 4.43 1.50 1.33 1.72 3.47 3. 1.20 1.22 1.31 2.40 3.09 3.12 0.301 0.335 0.266 0.327 0.372 0.249 0.258 0.238 0.316 0.363 0.315 0.407 0.319 0.326 0.385 0. 0.273 0.241 0.180 0.046 0.106 0.362 0.427 0.154 0.121 0.113 0.223 0.171 0.064 0.040 0.054 0.065 Table 1: Comparison on CelebV-HQ easy (same-video), hard (cross-video), and TalkVid splits. higher is better, lower is better. Best in bold, second best underlined. Commercial model. 4.3 Evaluation Metrics We evaluate five axes against the GT target videos: Speaker Similarity via WavLM+ECAPATDNN Wang et al. [2021] cosine similarity Face Similarity via ArcFace Deng et al. [2019] cosine similarity (5-frame average); Lip Synchronization via SyncNet Chung and Zisserman [2016], Prajwal et al. [2020], LSE-D () and LSE-C (); Audio Prompt Adherence via CLAP Elizalde et al. [2023] similarity between generated audio and the combined environment and speaking style prompt; and WER via Whisper-large-v3 Radford et al. [2022] transcription. Full details are provided in the supplementary material. 4.4 Implementation Details We implement ID-LoRA on top of the LTX-2 model using LoRA with rank 128. Training is performed on single NVIDIA H100 GPU for 6,000 steps, using AdamW optimizer with learning rate 2104 and batch size of 4. First-frame conditioning is applied with probability 0.9 during training. At inference, we use 30 denoising steps with the following guidance scales: video CFG = 3.0, audio CFG = 7.0, identity guidance = 4.0, and AV-bimodal CFG = 3.0. STG (Spatio-Temporal Guidance) is applied with scale 1.0 on block 29. Generated videos are 10241024 resolution with 121 frames at 25fps for both CelebV-HQ and TalkVid. AV-bimodal and STG guidance have been introduced in HaCohen et al. [2026] and Hyung et al. [2024] respectively.

sections.title: 5 Results
sections.content: 5.1 Main Results on CelebV-HQ Table 1 presents our main comparison between ID-LoRA, three cascaded baselines (CosyVoice 3.0 + WAN2.2, VoiceCraft + WAN2.2, and ElevenLabs + WAN2.2), and the state-of-the-art closedsource Kling 2.6 Pro model across all evaluation splits: CelebV-HQ easy (same-video), CelebV-HQ hard (cross-video), and TalkVid. All cascaded baselines use WAN2.2 Wan et al. [2025] as the video backbone. We additionally isolate architecture effects by comparing against LTX-based variants in Table 2. On the easy (same-video) subset, all methods benefit from high source-target similarity, representing voice replication scenario. On the hard (cross-video) subset, which tests generalization to novel

Best in bold, second best underlined. acoustic settings, our advantages become more pronounced: the speaker similarity gap over the best cascaded baseline widens from +0.063 (easy) to +0.086 (hard). This suggests that our unified approach generalizes more robustly to new settings, whereas cascaded pipelines degrade more sharply when reference and target conditions diverge. ID-LoRA outperforms all cascaded baselines on speaker similarity and lip synchronization, demonstrating that unified generation scheme better preserves the target voice and produces more coherent audio-visual alignment than cascading separately trained models. Kling 2.6 Pro, unified commercial solution trained on significantly larger data, does not surpass our open approach on these axes. Both unified approaches (ours and Kling) also achieve substantially higher audio prompt adherence (CLAP) than the cascaded baselines, with the gap widening on the hard split (+0.105 vs. best cascaded) where environments diverge and cascaded pipelines blindly propagate reference acoustics. Statistical significance tests confirm our speaker similarity gains over all baselines at 0.001, and our CLAP advantage over Kling reaches p0.01. 5.2 Cross-Dataset Generalization We additionally report TalkVid results in Table 1. ID-LoRA maintains its advantages in speaker similarity and lip synchronization on this dataset. Notably, the CelebV-HQ checkpoint applied to TalkVid without fine-tuning achieves speaker similarity of 0.595, only 0.004 below the in-domain checkpoint, still surpassing all other baselines, while actually improving CLAP (0.412 vs. 0.385) and LSE-D (10.32 vs. 10.62). This likely reflects CelebV-HQs greater acoustic diversity, indicating that ID-LoRA learns transferable identity representations rather than dataset-specific shortcuts. 5.3 Controlling for Backbone Effects To isolate pipeline design from model family effects, Table 2 compares ID-LoRA with LTX-based cascaded variants (CosyVoice-LTX, VoiceCraft-LTX, ElevenLabs-LTX) and LTX-Zeroshot. Across CelebV-HQ easy/hard, ID-LoRA remains the strongest on speaker similarity and synchronization-oriented metrics (LSE-C and competitive LSE-D), while several LTX variants achieve higher face similarity, an effect we analyze below. On TalkVid, ID-LoRA still leads in speaker similarity and low WER, whereas LTX-Zeroshot can obtain very low LSE-D together with degraded transcription quality (high WER), indicating that low SyncNet distance alone can over-

PEs + IdG) 0.438 0.441 0.477 0.875 0.872 0.874 8.71 9.02 8. 0.364 0.359 0.363 0.113 0.252 0.113 Table 3: Core design ablation on the CelebV-HQ hard split (cross-video): no identity guidance, standard positional encoding, and full OURS (negative positions + identity guidance). The full setup gives the best overall balance on synchronization and intelligibility while retaining strong identity. (a) ID-LoRA vs. Kling 2.6 Pro (b) ID-LoRA vs. ElevenLabs + Wan 2.2 Figure 3: Human evaluation results: A/B preference rates (%) on the hard (cross-video) split. Annotators on AMT evaluated 35 pairs across 8 speakers along three axes: voice similarity, environment sounds, and speech manners. estimate quality when articulation is poor. This motivates evaluating synchronization jointly with intelligibility and identity rather than in isolation. Face similarity and lip synchronization. All baselines achieve comparable or higher face similarity than ID-LoRA, while Kling 2.6 Pro scores lower (0.8470.854). This reflects systematic bias in ArcFace: methods with less lip motion keep face embeddings closer to the reference, inflating the metric. similar confound affects lip synchronization: LTX-Zeroshot achieves the lowest LSE-D across all splits yet also the lowest LSE-C confidence and largely unintelligible speech (WER > 2.3 on CelebV-HQ), indicating that its low lip-sync distance stems from near-static frames rather than accurate articulation. These observations motivate evaluating synchronization jointly with intelligibility and identity rather than in isolation, and suggest that face similarity alone is an incomplete measure for talking-head generation, as it penalizes realistic speech articulation. quantitative lip motion analysis is provided in the supplementary material. 5.4 Ablation Studies Negative Temporal Positions and Identity-guidance. Table 3 presents core design ablation comparing 3 configurations on the hard split. Replacing negative temporal positions with standard positional encodings degrades lip synchronization (LSE-D: 9.02 vs. 8.50) and sharply increases WER (0.252 vs. 0.113), indicating that the model struggles to separate reference from target speech without clear positional boundary. Removing identity guidance reduces speaker similarity (0.438 vs. 0.477), confirming that identity guidance steers the model toward speaker-specific features. sensitivity analysis of the identity guidance scale is provided in the supplementary material. 5.5 Human Evaluation To complement automatic metrics, we conduct two human evaluations on Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT), with 9 annotators per item. Participation is restricted to workers with Masters qualification, HIT approval rate above 97%, and location in English-speaking countries. 9 Box Thud Glass Break Metal Box Drums Hand Clap Jackhammer Knocking Tree Fall Bird Chirp Guitar Figure 4: Examples from the MOS evaluation set. Each column shows two generated videos from the same speaker under different environmental sound conditions. Figure 5: Environment sound interaction MOS study: per-scenario mean opinion scores (15) with 95% confidence intervals for ID-LoRA and Kling 2.6 Pro across ten physical interaction scenarios. ID-LoRA scores higher on 8 of 10 scenarios and in overall score. A/B Preference Study. We perform two A/B preference tests comparing ID-LoRA against Kling 2.6 Pro and ElevenLabs + WAN2.2, using the CelebV-HQ hard split. For each pair, annotators are shown videos from both methods in randomized A/B order and select preference (A, B, or Equal) along three axes: voice similarity (with reference video shown), environment sounds (with the target description shown), and speech manners (with the target style description shown). To ensure annotation quality, we employ A/B randomization independent per (pair, question), cyclic Latin square so that no annotator evaluates the same pair under two different question types, watch-gating that disables rating until all videos are watched to completion, guided practice rounds, and two hidden control pairs with known answers to filter inattentive workers (see supplementary for full protocol). Figure 3 shows the preference rates, with 285290 annotations per question type. All p-values are from two-sided binomial tests on win counts excluding equal responses. Against ElevenLabs + Wan 2.2, ID-LoRA is overwhelmingly preferred for voice similarity (80.7% vs. 17.5%; win rate 82.1%, < 0.001) and environment sounds (68.7% vs. 5.6%; win rate 92.4%, < 0.001), with moderate advantage for speech manners (55.5% vs. 39.9%; win rate 58.1%, < 0.01). Against Kling 2.6 Pro, ID-LoRA is significantly preferred on all three axes (p < 0.001): voice similarity (73.1% vs. 20.0%; win rate 78.5%), environment sounds (54.8% vs. 20.7%; win rate 72.6%), and speech manners (65.2% vs. 30.7%; win rate 68.0%). Across both comparisons, the strongest advantages appear in voice similarity and environment sounds, validating that unified generation jointly preserves speaker identity and enables text-driven control over the audio scene. Environment Sound Interaction MOS Study. The A/B study evaluates ambient sounds described in the prompt (e.g., wind, traffic). more demanding test is whether the model can generate sounds corresponding to physical interactions depicted in the scenee.g., thud when box drops or guitar music when strumming. Cascaded baselines cannot perform this task as they generate speech only; we therefore compare ID-LoRA against Kling 2.6 Pro only. We design ten interaction 10 scenarios, e.g. dropping box, clapping, drumming. For each scenario, we select five speakers from TalkVid and use Nano-banana Google DeepMind [2026] to insert scene-appropriate object and background into the first frame (e.g., guitar in the speakers hands). Both methods generate videos from these edited frames with prompts describing the interaction, yielding new 50 paired samples. Annotators rate each video independently on 15 MOS scale (1 = Bad, 5 = Excellent) assessing how well the generated audio matches the depicted interaction. Representative examples are shown in Fig. 4; Scenario details and per-scenario analysis are in the supplementary. Results are shown in Figure 5. ID-LoRA achieves higher overall MOS (3.05 vs. 2.90), wins on 8 of 10 scenarios, and exhibits lower variance with fewer Bad ratings (15.3% vs. 23.3%). The fact that ID-LoRA, trained on only 3K pairs, matches large-scale commercial system confirms that unified generation provides strong inductive bias for physically grounded audio-visual correspondence.

sections.title: 6 Conclusions
sections.content: We presented ID-LoRA, unified audio-visual personalization method that jointly synthesizes subjects appearance and vocal identity. Unlike cascaded pipelines, our approach benefits from cross-modal attention and allows text conditioning to influence both modalities simultaneously by leveraging the LTX-2 backbone for zero-shot personalization. Key architectural contributions include negative temporal positions for reference-target separation and identity guidance for enhanced speaker preservation. Experiments demonstrate that ID-LoRA outperforms cascaded baselines and competes with stateof-the-art closed-source models in speaker similarity and lip synchronization. Crucially, human evaluations show our method significantly surpasses both cascaded baselines and Kling 2.6 Pro in matching speech manners, generating appropriate environment sounds, and integrating physically grounded audio. Overall, ID-LoRA yields more coherent audio-visual outputs with stronger prompt adherence and identity preservation than dedicated TTS pipelines. Future work will explore multispeaker, cross-lingual, and general Audio-Visual Reference-to-Video generation.

sections.title: Appendix
sections.content: This Appendix provides additional details and results that complement the main paper. We organize the content as follows: Section A: Additional implementation details. Section B: Additional quantitative results. Section C: Additional qualitative results. Section D: Human evaluation details. Section E: Broader impact and ethics.

sections.title: A Additional Implementation Details
sections.content: A.1 Dataset Details Training Data. We train ID-LoRA on two datasets with separate checkpoints: 1. CelebV-HQ Zhu et al. [2022]: large-scale high-quality video dataset containing diverse talking-head videos with varied acoustic environments and speaking styles. 2. TalkVid Chen et al. [2025]: large-scale dataset of upper-body talking-head videos spanning 7,729 speakers across 15+ languages, featuring high-resolution footage (1080p 4K) curated through multi-stage pipeline. TalkVid exhibits less acoustic diversity than CelebV-HQ; its videos are predominantly recorded in controlled settings with fewer background sounds and less environmental variation. Both datasets are preprocessed through the following pipeline: 1. Filtering videos without audio or with insufficient speech; 2. Trimming leading and trailing silence; 3. Segmenting into 121-frame clips at 25fps (4.8 seconds); 4. Generating video captions (visual and speech) and audio-only captions (speaking style and environmental sounds) via Gemini; 5. Retaining only English-language samples; 6. Clustering speakers using face embeddings; 7. Retaining only speakers with 2 segments. Pair Construction. We then construct reference-target pairs from each speakers clip pool. For CelebV-HQ, we balance the training set to contain 50% same-video pairs and 50% cross-video pairs, encouraging the model to generalize across both settings, and filter out pairs whose speaker similarity falls below 0.45 (measured via WavLM+ECAPA-TDNN), ensuring that the model trains only on pairs with reliable identity correspondence. For TalkVid, we form same-speaker reference-target pairs from the resulting 752 speaker clusters (7,862 segments). We apply Silero VAD filtering to remove segments where source separation inadvertently stripped speech. For both datasets, we then partition speakers into disjoint train and test sets (approximately 80/20 split), ensuring no speaker identity leakage between splits. The resulting training set comprises approximately 3,000 pairs across 1,295 speakers for CelebV-HQ and 5,796 pairs across 600 speakers for TalkVid. Source Separation. Critically, we apply source separation to strip background sounds from all reference audio clips, providing clean speech-only references during training. This prevents the model from simply copying the reference environment into the output and instead forces it to rely on the text prompt for environment sounds and speaking style, which is essential for prompt-adherent audio generation. 15 Evaluation Splits. For CelebV-HQ, we curate two evaluation splits from held-out set of speakers not seen during training: Easy (same-video): reference and target clips come from different segments of the same source video. This setting represents voice replication with high source-target speaker similarity. Hard (cross-video): reference and target clips come from different videos of the same speaker. This tests generalization to new videos where acoustic conditions and speaking style vary significantly between source and target, resulting in lower baseline speaker similarity. Overall, we curate 35 video pairs in the hard split, and 36 video pairs in the easy split. For TalkVid, we curate quiet-to-environment benchmark from held-out speakers not seen during training. Each pair consists of quiet (clean) reference clip and target clip containing environmental sounds, testing whether the model can generate appropriate environment sounds from clean reference while preserving speaker identity. The final set contains 49 pairs across 41 speakers (18 same-video, 31 cross-video). Unlike the train set, the test splits did not go through source separation to keep the evaluation dataset similar to real-world data distribution. A.2 Baseline Details We compare ID-LoRA against three cascaded pipelines and unified commercial model. We note that Audiobox Vyas et al. [2023], while the closest audio-only work to ours, is closed source and currently unavailable, precluding direct comparison. Cascaded Baselines. All three cascaded baselines follow two-stage pattern: zero-shot voicecloning model generates speech from reference audio clip and text transcript, then soundconditioned image-to-video model (WAN2.2 Wan et al. [2025]) produces the final video from this audio and first-frame image. We evaluate three TTS models: CosyVoice 3.0 Du et al. [2024]: flow-based multilingual voice cloning model. VoiceCraft Peng et al. [2024]: an autoregressive neural codec language model for zeroshot speech editing and TTS. ElevenLabs ElevenLabs [2026]: state-of-the-art commercial voice cloning platform with built-in emotion and style control. We applied their automatic prompt enhancement to adjust pacing, emphasis, and emotion following their official recommendations, making it the strongest cascaded baseline for speaking style adherence. Kling 2.6 Pro. Kling 2.6 Pro Kuaishou Technology [2026] is closed-source commercial model that generates talking-face video with voice cloning in unified (non-cascaded) pipeline. It represents strong industry baseline for audio-visual personalization. A.3 Evaluation Metric Details Speaker Similarity. We measure speaker identity preservation using WavLM Large Chen et al. [2022a] combined with ECAPA-TDNN Desplanques et al. [2020], following the UniSpeech-SAT speaker verification framework Chen et al. [2022b]. We report cosine similarity between generated and target audio embeddings. Face Similarity. Visual identity is measured using ArcFace Deng et al. [2019] embeddings extracted from detected faces. We average embeddings across 5 uniformly sampled frames and report cosine similarity to the target face. Lip Synchronization. Audio-visual alignment is evaluated using SyncNet Chung and Zisserman [2016] metrics: LSE-D (Lip Sync Error - Distance, lower is better) and LSE-C (Lip Sync Error - Confidence, higher is better). These metrics quantify how well lip movements match the generated audio. 16 Table 4: Lip motion (102) vs. face similarity (FaceSim) for the top 30% highest-FaceSim samples of each method. Higher lip motion indicates more active mouth articulation. Across all datasets, methods with less lip movement consistently achieve higher FaceSim, revealing systematic metric bias against realistic speech animation. CelebVHQ-Hard CelebVHQ-Easy TalkVid Method LipMot. FaceSim LipMot. FaceSim LipMot. FaceSim LTX-Zeroshot VoiceCraft-MFA-LTX CosyVoice-LTX ElevenLabs-LTX Ours 0.95 1.03 1.07 1.29 1. 0.947 0.944 0.944 0.943 0.936 1.73 0.97 1.90 1.29 2.98 0.944 0.947 0.940 0.942 0. 2.41 2.94 2.95 2.82 3.07 0.927 0.832 0.828 0.829 0.832 Audio Prompt Adherence. To evaluate how faithfully the generated audio follows the text prompts intended environment and speaking style, we compute CLAP Elizalde et al. [2023] similarity between the generated audio and the combined prompt description of environment sounds and speaking style. Higher CLAP scores indicate better adherence to the prompt-specified audio properties. In cascaded pipelines, the voice-cloning stage tends to override these prompt-specified properties with the reference clips acoustics; our unified approach enables the text prompt to influence the generated audio directly. Word Error Rate (WER). We measure speech intelligibility by transcribing the generated audio using Whisper-large-v3 and computing WER against the ground-truth transcript. Lower WER indicates clearer, more intelligible speech generation.

sections.title: B Additional Quantitative Results
sections.content: B.1 Face Similarity and Lip Motion Analysis All baselines achieve comparable or higher face similarity than ID-LoRA, while Kling 2.6 Pro, another unified model, scores even lower (0.8470.854). To investigate whether this gap stems from reduced facial dynamics rather than superior identity preservation, we measure lip motion by extracting InsightFace Deng et al. [2019] 68-point landmarks on every frame, computing the perframe normalized inner mouth opening, and reporting its standard deviation over time. As shown in Table 4, focusing on the top 30% of samples by face similarity for each LTX method, our method consistently exhibits the highest lip motion across all three benchmarks while achieving the lowest FaceSim, and conversely LTX-Zeroshot, which produces no speech-driven animation, achieves the lowest lip motion yet the highest FaceSim by wide margin (0.927 vs. 0.832 on TalkVid). Qualitatively, we observe that the LTX-based cascaded pipelines frequently produce near-static frames with little to no facial movement, which trivially preserves the reference identity. This reveals systematic bias in ArcFace-based face similarity: less facial deformation keeps the face embedding closer to the target identity, inflating the metric independently of actual identity preservation quality. Face similarity alone is therefore an incomplete measure for talking-head video generation, as it inherently penalizes methods that produce more realistic speech articulation. B.2 Identity-guidance sensitivity sweep Identity Guidance Scale. Table 5 analyzes the effect of the identity guidance scale sid on the hard split. At the lowest tested scale (sid = 2), speaker similarity drops substantially (0.459 vs. 0.477 at sid = 4), confirming that identity guidance is critical for voice preservation. Increasing the scale beyond the default (sid = 4) further improves speaker similarity up to sid = 10 (0.485), but with degrading lip synchronization and speech intelligibility at high scales. We select sid = 4 as the default, balancing speaker identity with generation quality. 17 Table 5: Identity-guidance scale sweep sid on the hard split (two-stage), with negative temporal positions enabled. Larger sid generally improves speaker similarity but introduces trade-offs in lipsync and ASR metrics. sid 2 4 (default) 6 8 10 12 14 16 Spk Sim Face Sim LSE-D CLAP WER 0.459 0.477 0.477 0.481 0.485 0.482 0.473 0.470 0.872 0.874 0.874 0.873 0.872 0.876 0.872 0.872 8.63 8.50 8.45 8.52 8.56 8.69 8.73 8. 0.348 0.363 0.355 0.361 0.353 0.350 0.358 0.358 0.117 0.113 0.142 0.185 0.119 0.145 0.147 0.

sections.title: C Additional Qualitative Results
sections.content: Please refer to our website https://id-lora.github.io/ for high-resolution video comparisons and qualitative examples

sections.title: D Human Evaluation Details
sections.content: D.1 A/B Preference Study Protocol We conduct two separate A/B preference tests on Amazon Mechanical Turk (AMT), each comparing ID-LoRA against one baseline: Kling 2.6 Pro and ElevenLabs + WAN2.2. All studies use the hard (cross-video) evaluation split comprising 35 video pairs across 8 held-out CelebV-HQ speakers. Each pair is evaluated by 9 annotators. We restrict participation to workers with Masters qualification, HIT approval rate above 97%, and location in English-speaking countries (US, UK, Canada, Australia). For each video pair, annotators are shown videos generated by ID-LoRA and the baseline in randomized A/B order and asked to select preference (A, B, or Equal) along three axes: 1. Voice Similarity: Which videos voice sounds more like the reference? (a reference video of the target speaker is shown alongside the two candidates). 2. Environment Sounds: Which videos background sounds better match the description? (the target environment sounds description is displayed). 3. Speech Manners: Which videos speaking style better matches the description? (the target speaking style description is displayed). The instruction pages for both studies are shown in Figure 6, and example evaluation cards are shown in Figure 7. Bias Prevention. We employ several mechanisms to ensure annotation quality: A/B randomization: The A/B assignment is randomized independently per (pair, question), so the same pair may have the methods in opposite positions for different questions. Cyclic Latin square: cyclic Latin square distributes pairs across annotators such that no annotator evaluates the same pair for two different question types, preventing familiarity bias. Watch-gating: Rating buttons are disabled until all videos have been watched to completion; forward-scrubbing is blocked on unanswered pairs. Practice rounds: Guided examples with Reveal Answer feedback are provided before the environment sounds and speech manners sections to calibrate annotators. Hidden control pairs: Two hidden control pairs with known correct answers (one for environment sounds, one for speech manners) are embedded in the evaluation to identify inattentive annotators. Workers who fail any control are excluded from analysis. (a) A/B preference study: annotators are briefed on the three evaluation axes and the practice round format. (b) MOS study: annotators rate how well the described non-speech sounds can be heard on 15 scale. Figure 6: Instruction pages shown to Amazon Mechanical Turk annotators at the beginning of each human evaluation study. D.2 Environment Sound Interaction MOS Study The A/B preference study evaluates ambient environment sounds described in the text prompt (e.g., wind, music, traffic). more demanding test of audio prompt adherence is whether the model can generate sounds corresponding to physical interactions described in the prompt and depicted in the scene for example, producing thud when the prompt describes box dropping, or guitar music when the prompt describes strumming. Why Only Unified Models. Cascaded voice-cloning baselines cannot perform this task: they generate clean speech only and have no mechanism to produce non-speech sounds. More broadly, any audio-only model generating without access to the evolving visual scene cannot accurately time acoustic events to on-screen actions: if bird is already mid-flight in the first frame, blind model has no visual cues about when the interaction begins or ends. Even Audiobox Vyas et al. [2023], which combines voice cloning with text-described background sounds, operates purely in the audio domain and cannot condition on visual context. This evaluation is therefore restricted to unified audio-visual models. We compare ID-LoRA against Kling 2.6 Pro as the state-of-the-art commercial unified model. Scenario Design. We design MOS study covering ten interaction scenarios: box dropping, clapping, drumming, glass breaking, guitar playing, jackhammer, door knocking, metal box hitting, bird chirping in park, and tree falling. For each scenario, we select five random speakers from the TalkVid dataset and use Nano Banana Pro Google DeepMind [2026] to edit the first frame of each speakers video to insert scene-appropriate object (e.g., guitar in the speakers hands, person working with jackhammer in the background). Both methods then generate videos from these edited first frames with text prompts describing the corresponding interaction sounds, yielding 50 paired comparisons. We collect 450 ratings per method from AMT annotators (9 annotators per video) on 15 MOS scale (1 = Bad, 5 = Excellent). The instruction page is shown in Figure 6b, and an example evaluation card is shown in Figure 7b. Per-Scenario Analysis. ID-LoRA achieves higher overall MOS than Kling 2.6 Pro (3.05 vs. 2.90) and wins on 8 of 10 scenarios, with the largest gains on bird chirping in park (+1.51), clapping (+0.42), and box dropping (+0.38). Kling outperforms on guitar (1.40) and drums (0.69), both sustained musical instrument sounds where its larger-scale training likely provides an advantage. ID-LoRA also exhibits lower variance and smaller proportion of Bad ratings (15.3% vs. 23.3%), suggesting more consistent generation quality. While the overall difference does not reach statistical significance (p = 0.093, Cohens = 0.115), the fact that ID-LoRA, parameter-efficient adaptation trained on 3K pairs, matches large19 (a) A/B preference study (voice similarity). Annotators watch reference video alongside two generated videos and select which voice sounds more similar, or equal. (b) MOS study (bird chirping scenario). Annotators see the sound description, watch the video, and rate the sound quality on 15 scale. Figure 7: Example evaluation cards from both human evaluation studies. scale commercial system confirms that unified generation provides powerful inductive bias for physically grounded audio-visual correspondence. We view this study as an exploratory first step toward evaluating diegetic consistency in unified audio-visual generation. By combining first-frame editing with unified generation, this protocol opens new directions for benchmarking physically grounded audio-visual correspondence, for example, testing whether reverberation adapts to scene geometry or whether impact sounds respect material properties, capabilities that are uniquely enabled by unified architectures.

sections.title: E Broader Impact and Ethics
sections.content: Potential Risks. ID-LoRA generates realistic audio-visual content that preserves specific persons face and voice from short reference clip. This capability, while valuable for legitimate applications, carries risks of misuse. Bad actors could produce non-consensual impersonations, fabricate misleading media, or clone persons likeness without authorization. Mitigations. We advocate for several safeguards to reduce misuse potential. First, generated content should carry invisible watermarks or provenance metadata (e.g., C2PA) to enable downstream detection and attribution. Second, deployment should require explicit consent from depicted individuals, particularly for voice cloning, where legal frameworks such as the EU AI Act increasingly mandate transparency and consent. Third, continued investment in deepfake detection research is essential to keep pace with generation advances. We note that ID-LoRA does not introduce fundamentally new risks beyond those already posed by existing commercial systems (Kling, ElevenLabs, HeyGen) that offer similar capabilities at scale; rather, it provides an open research framework for studying and mitigating these risks. Positive Applications. Unified audio-visual personalization enables several beneficial use cases: multilingual dubbing that preserves speakers identity and matches scene acoustics, accessibility tools that generate personalized videos, digital avatars for individuals with speech impairments, and creative content production where actors can be depicted in scenes that would be impractical or unsafe to film. 20 Data and Human Evaluation Ethics. Our training datasets (CelebV-HQ and TalkVid) are publicly available research datasets. Our human evaluation studies on Amazon Mechanical Turk followed standard ethical practices: annotators were compensated above minimum wage, participation was voluntary, and no personally identifiable information was collected beyond anonymized worker IDs. The study was designed to minimize annotator burden through watch-gating and practice rounds.
